verses.commentary: Another wife after Sarah's death. Keturah means incense or perfume. This is likely later in his life.
Six sons. Midian becomes significant (Midianites, Moses' father in law). Abraham's descendants multiply through multiple lines.
Further generations. Arabian tribes. Abraham's influence spreads.
Midianite clans. All from Keturah. The genealogy establishes connections.
Clear inheritance. Everything to Isaac. The covenant line is prioritized.
Gifts but not inheritance. Sent them away. To the land of the east, away from Canaan. Isaac's preeminence is protected. The other sons receive blessing but not the covenant promises.
Abraham's age. 175 years. A long, full life.
Abraham's death. Breathed his last, expired. Good old age. Old and full of years. Gathered to his people, a euphemism for death and perhaps reunion with ancestors. Peaceful death.
Isaac and Ishmael together. Reconciliation at the graveside. In the cave of Machpelah, with Sarah. The field Abraham purchased (chapter 23).
Emphasis on legal purchase. Abraham and Sarah together. The patriarchs will all rest here.
God blessed Isaac. The blessing continues to the next generation. Near Beer Lahai Roi, the well of the Living One who sees me (16:14). Continuity.
Ishmael's line. The account (toledot). Though not the covenant line, Ishmael receives attention.
Twelve sons as promised (17:20). Nebaioth. Kedar (mentioned in prophets as Arabian tribes).
More sons. Arabian tribes and territories.
Final sons. Twelve total. God's promise to Hagar and Abraham fulfilled.
Twelve tribal rulers, princes. Settlements and camps. They become a great nation as promised.
Ishmael's death. 137 years. Breathed his last. Gathered to his people. He receives honorable mention.
Geographic range. From Havilah to Shur. Near Egypt. Toward Ashur. Lived in hostility, fulfilling the prophecy (16:12). Tension with relatives, especially Israel.
Isaac's account begins. Abraham fathered Isaac. The covenant line continues.
Isaac marries at 40. Rebekah's lineage repeated. Paddan Aram, Mesopotamia. Laban mentioned, foreshadowing Jacob's story.
Barrenness again. Isaac prayed. The pattern repeats (Sarah, Rebekah, later Rachel). The LORD answered. Rebekah conceives. Prayer and divine intervention.
Struggle in the womb. Jostled, crushed, struggled. Why is this happening? She inquires of the LORD. Seeking understanding.
Prophetic word. Two nations. Two peoples. Separated. One stronger. The older will serve the younger. This reverses normal primogeniture. Edom and Israel. Jacob and Esau. God's sovereign choice.
Birth. Twin boys. The prophecy begins to unfold.
First born. Red (admoni). Hairy. Named Esau, possibly meaning hairy or made/complete. Edom (red) will be his other name.
Second born. Hand grasping the heel. Named Jacob, heel-grabber or supplanter. The struggle begins at birth. Isaac was 60. Twenty years of waiting after marriage.
Different personalities. Esau the hunter, outdoorsman. Jacob stayed among tents, domestic, contemplative. The differences will create conflict.
Parental favoritism. Isaac loved Esau because of food. Rebekah loved Jacob. Divided loyalties. Trouble brewing.
Scene set. Jacob cooking. Esau returns famished, weary, exhausted.
Desperation. Quick. Red stew. Edom, his name. The wordplay on red continues.

Jacob's condition. Sell me your birthright. The right of the firstborn: double portion, family leadership, covenant blessing. Jacob wants what God has already promised (verse 23) but seeks it through manipulation.
Esau's exaggeration. About to die. Hyperbole. What good is the birthright? He despises it. Values immediate gratification over future blessing.
Jacob insists on an oath. Legal transaction. Esau swore. Selling his birthright. Foolish trade.

The meal. Bread and lentil stew. He ate, drank, left. So Esau despised his birthright. He treated it with contempt. Hebrews 12:16 calls him godless for this. Short-term thinking. Spiritual insensitivity. The stage is set for ongoing conflict between the brothers and their descendants.
